questions.question: Tell me about yourself
questions.answer:
Structure your answer as: Present → Past → Future (2-3 minutes max).

Template:

"I'm a .NET developer with about 5 years of experience, currently [role/looking for]. I work primarily with ASP.NET Core, Angular, and SQL Server — building web applications from the API layer all the way to the frontend.

Before that, I worked on [briefly mention key projects/responsibilities]. One of the projects I'm most proud of is [FolioTrack/offline invoice/etc.] where I built [specific feature] that [specific impact].

I'm now looking for [type of role] where I can [growth goal — work on larger scale systems / deepen expertise in cloud / lead technically]. I'm particularly interested in this role because [specific reason about the company/tech stack]."

Key principles:

  • Keep it professional — no personal life details unless relevant
  • Lead with current state, not your whole career history
  • Mention 1-2 specific achievements (with numbers if possible)
  • End with why you're here — connects you to this company
  • Practice until it's smooth — don't read from a script but don't ramble
followUps: Why are you looking to change jobs?
What are you looking for in your next role?

followUpAnswers.Why are you looking to change jobs?:
This question tests your honesty, self-awareness, and professionalism. Interviewers want to know you're leaving for growth, not running away from problems — and they want to make sure you're not a flight risk or someone who badmouths employers.

Principles:

  • Be honest but professional — you can be direct without being negative
  • Frame it as moving toward something, not fleeing from something
  • Never criticize your current/previous employer, manager, or teammates by name
  • Mention positive things about your current role before explaining the gap

Sample answers (choose what fits your situation):

For growth reasons: "I've genuinely enjoyed my time at [company] — I've learned a lot and delivered real results, including [specific achievement]. That said, the team and product have stabilised, and the opportunities to take on new technical challenges have become limited. I'm at a point in my career where I want to work on larger-scale systems and deepen my expertise in [cloud / distributed systems / senior design patterns], and this role looks like the right context for that."

For tech stack reasons: "My current role is heavily focused on legacy systems, and while I've become very good at maintaining and improving them, my personal development in modern .NET and Angular has been self-driven outside of work hours. I want to be in an environment where the work itself helps me grow — where I'm using modern tooling as a team standard, not as an exception."

For culture/scope reasons (without negativity): "My current team is small and I've valued the ownership that comes with that. I'm now looking for a team where I can collaborate more — learn from experienced engineers and contribute to a product at a bigger scale."

What to avoid:

  • "My manager is terrible" — never
  • "I want more money" — even if true, don't lead with it
  • "I got bored" — reframe as "seeking more challenge"
  • Giving a long complaint list — one clear reason is better than five grievances
followUpAnswers.What are you looking for in your next ro:
This question lets you communicate what motivates you and gives the interviewer a chance to sell their role to you. It also reveals whether your expectations are realistic and aligned with what they offer.

Four dimensions to address (pick 2-3 that are genuine for you):

  • Technical challenge: The kind of problems you want to work on
  • Growth: What you want to learn or the direction you want to grow
  • Team: The type of culture and collaboration environment
  • Impact: The scale or meaningfulness of the work

Sample answer:

"There are a few things I'm looking for. First, I want to work on systems that have real scale and complexity — not just CRUD apps, but problems like performance under load, concurrent users, or interesting domain logic. I've gotten a taste of that in [project] and want more.

Second, I want to be in a team where code quality is taken seriously — where there's peer review, where architectural decisions are discussed, and where I can learn from engineers who are more experienced than I am. I'm at a stage where the team around me matters a lot for my growth.

And lastly, I'm looking for a role where I can eventually take on more responsibility — lead a feature area, mentor junior developers, have input on technical decisions. I'm not expecting that on day one, but I want to know the path exists."

Tailor to the company: After giving your answer, you can flip it: "Does that align with what this role offers?" This invites the interviewer to pitch the role to you and shows you're thinking critically, not just saying yes to everything.

questions.tip: Customize the ending for each company. Research the tech stack, team size, product, and mention specifically why this company/role aligns with your goals. Generic "I want to grow" endings are forgettable.

questions.question: Tell me about a challenging project you worked on and how you handled it
questions.answer:
Use the STAR framework: Situation → Task → Action → Result.

Example answer (PaymentReminder project):

Situation: We had a PaymentReminder background service in .NET 8 that was supposed to send reminder emails to clients with overdue invoices. The service worked fine in dev but in production it was occasionally sending duplicate reminders to the same client — causing client complaints.

Task: I needed to identify why duplicates were happening and fix it without disrupting the service (it ran every hour).

Action: I added structured logging (Serilog) to trace each email send with the invoice ID and timestamp. The logs revealed the issue: when two server instances ran simultaneously (after a scale-out), both would pick up the same invoices from the DB because there was no distributed lock. I implemented an idempotency approach — marking invoices as "reminder-sent" in a DB update within the same transaction as the email send. I also added a unique constraint on the reminder tracking table so duplicate records were impossible at the DB level.

Result: Zero duplicate reminders in the 4 months since the fix. The extra logging also helped us catch other issues faster.
followUps: What did you learn from it?

followUpAnswers.What did you learn from it?:
This follow-up tests your ability to reflect and extract lessons — a strong signal of professional maturity. The best answers are specific (not generic platitudes) and show the lesson changed your approach going forward.

What good answers look like:

  • A technical lesson tied to the specific problem
  • A process lesson (something you now do differently in your workflow)
  • A communication lesson (how you worked with others differently)

Sample answer (continuing from PaymentReminder):

"The biggest technical lesson was about designing for distributed execution from the start. I had assumed we'd always run one instance of the service. The moment we scaled out, an assumption I'd never examined became a bug. Now I always ask: 'what happens if two instances of this run at the same time?' before writing any background job logic.

I also learned the value of structured, correlation-ID-based logging. Before this incident, my logging was adequate. After adding proper tracing — with invoice IDs and timestamps in every log line — I could replay exactly what happened and why. It turned a confusing production incident into a 30-minute root-cause analysis.

On the process side, I learned to push for load testing of background services, not just HTTP endpoints. We tested the API under load but had never simulated the service running on multiple nodes. That's now part of how I think about testing any background processing."

Tip: Avoid generic answers like "I learned communication is important" or "I learned to test more." Ground every lesson in a specific change to how you work.
followUpAnswers.What would you do differently?:
This is the hardest follow-up because it requires genuine self-critique. Interviewers are testing whether you can be honest with yourself — not whether you made mistakes (everyone does), but whether you can identify them and reason about them clearly.

Principles:

  • Be specific — name a real decision you'd change, not a vague regret
  • Show the insight — why you'd do it differently, not just that you would
  • Don't over-apologize or beat yourself up — own it and move forward
  • Don't choose something trivial ("I'd comment my code better") — show real stakes

Sample answer (continuing from PaymentReminder):

"Honestly, I would have pushed harder to add idempotency infrastructure before the problem surfaced. When I first built the reminder service, I considered whether we'd ever run multiple instances and told myself 'we don't scale this service, so it's not needed.' That was a pragmatic shortcut that turned into technical debt.

Looking back, adding an idempotency key — just a GUID per reminder job — would have taken maybe two hours when I was building it fresh. Fixing it in production, with live clients getting duplicates, cost a weekend of debugging and a difficult conversation with the customer success team. The cost-benefit was clear in hindsight.

What I'd do differently: treat background services with the same distributed-systems paranoia as any other component. The rule I've adopted since is: any service that writes data or sends external messages gets an idempotency mechanism by default."

Structure: State the decision → Explain why you made it at the time → Explain what you'd change → Show the principle you extracted.

questions.tip: STAR answers should be specific, not generic. "We had a bug, I fixed it" is not a story. The interviewer wants to understand your problem-solving process, how you communicate under pressure, and how you handle ambiguity.

questions.question: Tell me about a conflict you had with a team member and how you resolved it
questions.answer:
Use STAR. Show maturity — you focused on the problem, not the person.

Key principles for conflict questions:

  • Never blame the other person — "they were wrong, I was right" is a red flag
  • Show you took initiative to resolve it, didn't wait for a manager
  • Focus on the outcome and what you both learned
  • Demonstrate empathy — you understood their perspective

Example structure:

"In [project], I disagreed with a colleague about [technical/process decision]. They wanted to [their approach], while I believed [your approach] was better because [technical reason].

Instead of escalating immediately, I set up a short one-on-one to understand their reasoning. It turned out they had [context I didn't know about — deadline pressure, constraint]. Once I understood their concern, we agreed to [compromise or you adjusted your view].

The outcome was [result]. I learned that [what you took away — importance of understanding context before disagreeing, how to frame technical disagreements as options with tradeoffs rather than right/wrong]."
followUps: How do you handle disagreements about technical decisions with senior developers?
What do you do when you strongly disagree with a decision but are outvoted?

followUpAnswers.How do you handle disagreements about te:
This question tests your judgment, communication skills, and ability to advocate for your ideas without being confrontational — one of the most important interpersonal skills for mid-level developers.

The right approach — frame it as a data/options conversation, not a disagreement:

  • Separate the idea from the person — you're challenging the approach, not the person's judgment or authority
  • Lead with curiosity, not challenge — "Help me understand the reasoning behind X" reveals context you might be missing
  • Present your alternative as one option with tradeoffs, not the correct answer
  • Write it down — a short document comparing options forces you to be precise and makes the conversation productive
  • Know when to yield — pick your battles; not every disagreement is worth pushing

Sample answer:

"I try to approach it as a collaborative problem-solving conversation rather than a debate I need to win. If I disagree with a senior developer's technical decision, the first thing I do is make sure I fully understand their reasoning — I might be missing context about constraints, history, or tradeoffs that influenced their choice.

Once I understand their perspective, if I still think there's a better approach, I'll write out a short comparison — what each approach looks like, the pros and cons of each, any risks I see. A written format tends to be less emotionally charged than a verbal debate, and it gives both of us something concrete to evaluate.

In practice, this has gone both ways. Sometimes the senior developer has context I was missing and their approach makes complete sense once I understand it. Other times they've considered my point and we've adjusted the design. And sometimes we've disagreed but their authority and experience wins — which I accept and commit to fully."
followUpAnswers.What do you do when you strongly disagre:
The phrase interviewers are listening for here is "disagree and commit" — a principle from Amazon's leadership principles that has become widely recognised. It signals professional maturity: you can advocate strongly for your position, then fully commit to executing the team's decision without passive resistance.

The right framework:

  1. Voice your concern clearly, once. Make your case with specifics: what risk you see, what evidence you have. Don't repeat yourself or escalate unnecessarily.
  2. Make sure you're heard. The goal is not to win — it's to ensure the decision-maker has all the information, including your concern. If they do and still disagree, that's their call to make.
  3. Accept and commit. Once the decision is made, execute it with the same energy you'd give your own choice. Half-hearted execution of a team decision hurts everyone.
  4. Document your concern (when appropriate). For significant decisions with real risk, a brief written note creates a record without being passive-aggressive. "I want to note my concern about X for our records" is professional.

Sample answer:

"I've been in that situation. In one case, I strongly advocated for using an async queue for a feature instead of synchronous processing, because I was worried about timeout risks under load. The team decided to go with the synchronous approach because it was simpler and the deadline was tight — and I was outvoted.

I committed to it fully and made sure the synchronous implementation was as robust as possible. I did document my concern briefly in the PR description — not to be difficult, but because if the timeout issue surfaced later, I wanted us to be able to revisit it quickly with context.

As it turned out, the timeout issue did surface in production a few months later — we hit it at a higher load than expected. We implemented the queue then, and having the earlier documentation made it a quick and non-political conversation. The outcome was fine; the lesson was that sometimes you have to let a risk materialise before the team is ready to invest in mitigating it."

questions.tip: "Disagree and commit" is a key phrase. If you voiced your concern clearly and the team decided otherwise, you commit fully to executing the decision. Passive resistance ("fine, we'll do it your way") is a red flag. Show you can advocate for your view and then move forward.

questions.question: Where do you see yourself in 5 years?
questions.answer:
Interviewers want to know: Are you serious about growing? Will you stay long enough to be worth hiring? Are your goals realistic?

Good answer structure:

"In 5 years, I see myself as a senior or lead .NET developer — someone who's not just writing code but helping define architecture decisions, mentoring junior developers, and having a technical influence on the product direction.

Short-term, in the next 1-2 years, I want to deepen my expertise in [cloud-native development / microservices / system design] and contribute to more complex, higher-scale systems than I've worked on so far.

Longer-term, I'm open to either a technical track (principal engineer, architect) or a hybrid technical lead role — I want to stay hands-on with code but also have more impact on the system design and team direction. This role seems like a great step toward that because [specific mention of what this company offers]."

Avoid:

  • "In 5 years I want your job" — presumptuous
  • "I want to be a manager" — unless they're hiring for that path
  • "I'm not sure" — signals lack of direction
followUps: What are your short-term career goals?
What skills are you actively developing?

followUpAnswers.What are your short-term career goals?:
"Short-term" typically means 6–18 months. This follow-up is more concrete than the 5-year question — interviewers want to know what you'll be working on soon, and whether this role serves it.

Good short-term goals for a .NET developer:

  • Contribute to a production system at meaningful scale (more users, more complexity, more team size than you've had)
  • Go deeper on a technical area: cloud architecture (Azure/AWS), system design, distributed systems, performance optimization
  • Earn a certification or deliver a specific type of project (e.g., lead a feature from design to deployment)
  • Grow into a more senior scope: lead code reviews, write technical specs, mentor a junior

Sample answer:

"In the next 12-18 months, my primary goal is to contribute at a level beyond just task completion — I want to be someone who can own a feature end-to-end, from the design conversation to production monitoring. That means being more proactive about understanding the 'why' behind requirements, not just implementing the spec.

On the technical side, I'm actively building my knowledge of distributed system design — specifically around patterns like event-driven architecture, CQRS, and how to design systems that stay reliable at scale. These come up naturally at the kind of scale your team operates at, which is part of why this role appeals to me.

And within this team specifically, I'd want to establish myself as someone who delivers reliably and raises the quality bar — through good code reviews, thoughtful documentation, and catching things in design that save us time later."
followUpAnswers.What skills are you actively developing?:
This tests whether you're genuinely learning or just saying you are. The best answers are specific: name the skill, describe how you're learning it, and ideally mention something concrete you've built or done with it.

Strong answer structure: Skill → Method of learning → Concrete evidence or application

Sample answer (tailor to your actual learning):

"There are a few areas I'm actively investing in right now.

The first is cloud-native development on Azure. I've been studying for the AZ-204 certification and building small projects using Azure Service Bus and Azure Container Apps. The certification gives me structure but the hands-on projects are where the real learning happens — I've been rebuilding one of my side projects to run as containers orchestrated in ACA instead of a monolith on a single VM.

The second is system design and distributed systems patterns. I've been reading 'Designing Data-Intensive Applications' by Kleppmann and working through system design cases — both classic interview-style problems and real architectures I've read about on engineering blogs like Uber, Slack, and Netflix. I want to be able to reason about consistency, availability, and partition tolerance trade-offs, not just use abstractions that hide them.

And ongoing, I'm keeping up with .NET and Angular release cycles — experimenting with new features in sandbox projects before applying them in production. .NET 9's performance improvements and Angular 19's zoneless change detection were both things I tested out before they were on my work radar."

Tip: Name at least one concrete artifact — a project, a PR, a certification exam date, a book you're partway through. Evidence beats claims.

questions.tip: Tie your 5-year goal to this role. Show that this job is a step toward your goal, not just a placeholder. This tells the interviewer you've thought about your career and that this role genuinely serves it.

questions.question: What is your greatest strength?
questions.answer:
Pick a strength that is: real (you can prove it), relevant to the role, and specific (not generic).

Bad answer: "I'm a hard worker and a good team player." (Everyone says this.)

Good answer structure:

"One of my strongest skills is [specific technical/problem-solving skill]. For example, in [project], I was able to [specific situation]. I approached it by [what you did], which resulted in [specific outcome].

More broadly, I'm good at [meta-skill — breaking down complex problems / communicating technical concepts to non-technical stakeholders / picking up new technologies quickly] and that's been valuable in projects like [brief reference]."

Strengths relevant for .NET developers:

  • Debugging and root-cause analysis
  • Translating business requirements into clean technical designs
  • Picking up new technologies quickly (Angular, Azure, etc.)
  • Writing maintainable, well-structured code
  • Performance optimization instincts
followUps: Can you give me a specific example of that strength?

followUpAnswers.Can you give me a specific example of th:
This is the natural follow-up when your strength answer wasn't specific enough, or when the interviewer wants to dig deeper. It's an invitation to deliver a STAR story that makes your strength concrete and memorable.

What makes a good strength example:

  • Stakes are real — there was something meaningful on the line (a deadline, a client, a broken system)
  • You show your thinking process, not just the outcome
  • The example is relevant to the kind of work this role involves
  • Numbers or specifics make it credible: "reduced by 40%", "three days before launch", "affecting 2,000 users"

Example: if your strength is debugging and root-cause analysis

"A concrete example: in my PaymentReminder project, we had an intermittent production bug where some clients were receiving duplicate reminder emails. The logs weren't pointing to an obvious cause — the system looked healthy.

My approach was to instrument the code with structured logging — adding correlation IDs and timestamps at every stage of the reminder pipeline — and then reproduce the conditions by simulating two service instances running simultaneously. Within a few hours of that structured approach, the logs showed clearly that both instances were reading the same invoice records before either had marked them as processed.

The fix involved adding a transactional idempotency check: the service now does a SELECT with a lock, marks the record, and only then sends the email — all in one database transaction. Duplicates dropped to zero and stayed there.

What I'm proud of isn't just the fix — it's the diagnostic method. Adding visibility first, then using that visibility to reason about the system. That's the instinct I bring to any hard production problem."
followUpAnswers.What's your greatest weakness?:
The worst answers are fake strengths disguised as weaknesses ("I just work too hard") or genuine disqualifying weaknesses stated without growth context. The best answers are honest, specific, and show active self-improvement.

Formula: Name the weakness → Explain how it manifests → Describe what you're doing about it → Show progress

What to avoid:

  • "I'm a perfectionist" — transparent and clichéd
  • "I work too hard" — not a real weakness
  • A core job skill ("I'm not great at writing code") — disqualifying
  • Something with no growth narrative — shows no self-awareness

Sample answers for a .NET developer:

Over-engineering: "My instinct is often to build a more complete or generic solution than the immediate problem requires. I'll start designing a plugin system when the spec asked for one new button. I've learned to recognise this pattern in myself now — I ask 'what's the simplest thing that solves this?' before I start. I've also started time-boxing the design phase: if I spend more than 30 minutes designing a feature, I check whether I'm over-thinking it. It's a work in progress, but I'm much better at shipping simple things than I was two years ago."

Public communication: "I'm more comfortable communicating in writing than verbally in large groups. In one-on-ones or small technical discussions, I'm confident. But presenting to a large group or speaking up in a big all-hands meeting — I've historically been quieter than I wanted to be. I've been working on this deliberately: I started a habit of preparing one thing to say in team meetings, even if it's a question, just to build the habit. I'm not fully there yet, but I'm more comfortable than I was."

Tip: The growth narrative is the most important part. Interviewers don't expect perfection — they want to hire people who are self-aware and actively developing.

questions.tip: Greatest weakness follow-up: Pick a real weakness you're actively working on — not a disguised strength ("I work too hard"). Example: "I sometimes spend too much time optimizing code before shipping. I've been working on recognizing when 'good enough' is the right call for the current stage."

questions.question: How do you handle tight deadlines or pressure?
questions.answer:
Interviewers want to know you don't panic, you communicate, and you make smart tradeoffs.

Good answer:

"When I'm under a tight deadline, the first thing I do is prioritize ruthlessly — what absolutely must ship vs what's nice-to-have. I've learned that trying to do everything at 80% quality is worse than doing the critical parts at 100% and deferring the rest.

I also communicate early. If I see a deadline is at risk, I tell the team or manager as soon as I'm aware — not the day before. Early warning gives us options (scope reduction, extra help, deadline extension). Silence until the last minute doesn't help anyone.

In [specific project], we had a tight deadline where [situation]. I [specific action — broke it down, cut scope, worked with the team]. We delivered [result — what shipped, what was deferred and why that was the right call].

The one thing I try to avoid under pressure is cutting corners on code quality in ways that create tech debt that takes 3x longer to fix later. I'd rather have a productive conversation about scope than ship something fragile."
followUps: Tell me about a time you missed a deadline.

followUpAnswers.Tell me about a time you missed a deadli:
This is a test of honesty and accountability. Almost every experienced developer has missed a deadline at some point. The interviewer doesn't expect you to say you never have — they expect you to own it, explain what happened, and show what you learned.

What they're listening for:

  • Accountability — you own your part without blaming others
  • Transparency — you communicated early (or acknowledge you should have)
  • Learning — you took something actionable away from it
  • No defensiveness — you don't spend the answer justifying why it wasn't really your fault

STAR answer structure:

Situation: "In a project where I was building an integration with a third-party API for invoice synchronization, I had estimated 5 days of work. It was a commitment I made to the PM at the start of a sprint."

Task: "My part was the data transformation and mapping layer between our models and the external API's schema."

Action: "On day 3, I realised the external API's documentation was significantly out of date — there were 12 undocumented edge cases in their response format that I was discovering through trial and error. I had two choices: flag it early and reset expectations, or try to power through and hope I finished in time. I chose the wrong option initially — I kept trying to power through for another day before raising the issue, which cost us a day we could have used to plan differently."

Result: "We missed the sprint goal by two days. The feature shipped but a day after the client demo. The demo had to be run with partially stubbed data, which wasn't ideal.

What I took from it: raise the flag as soon as you see the estimate is wrong, not when it's already too late to adjust. The PM said later that if I'd told her on day 3, we could have moved the demo or deprioritised another story to give me more time. One conversation on day 3 would have prevented the miss. That's now a rule I follow: if I'm more than 20% through a task and the estimate feels wrong, I say something."

questions.tip: If you missed a deadline: Own it, explain what happened, what you learned, and what you'd do differently. Don't blame external factors. Accountability and learning from mistakes is valued more than perfection.

questions.question: Why do you want to work here?
questions.answer:
This question tests: Did you research us? Do you actually want this job or are we just option #12?

Answer formula: Company-specific reason + Role-specific fit + Your goal alignment

Research angles:

  • Tech stack — "I've been using .NET and SQL Server for 5 years, and I see your team is building on these. I can contribute immediately while also learning [their specific tech]."
  • Product — "I've been following [company's product] for a while. The [specific feature/challenge] is technically interesting to me because [genuine reason]."
  • Team/culture — "The engineering blog post about [specific topic] showed how seriously you take [code quality / engineering culture / specific practice]. That's the kind of team I want to work with."
  • Scale/challenge — "The scale at which you operate [millions of users, high throughput] is the kind of engineering challenge I want to work on next."

What to avoid:

  • "You pay well" (even if true)
  • "It's close to my home"
  • Generic "great company culture" without specifics
followUps: What do you know about our product/company?
What makes you a better fit than other candidates?

followUpAnswers.What do you know about our product/compa:
This is a direct test of whether you did your homework. Failing this question — giving a vague or generic answer — is one of the fastest ways to leave a bad impression, because it signals you either didn't care enough to research or couldn't retain what you read.

What to research before any interview (30-minute minimum):

  • Product: What does the company actually sell or build? Who are their customers? What problem do they solve? Use the product if you can.
  • Tech stack: Check the job description, LinkedIn employee profiles, engineering blog, StackShare, GitHub if they're open source
  • Company stage: Startup, growth stage, public company? Funded? Recent news?
  • Engineering culture: Engineering blog posts, tech talks on YouTube, Glassdoor reviews from engineers
  • Recent developments: New product launches, funding rounds, blog posts from the past 6 months

Sample answer structure:

"From my research, [Company] builds [core product description]. What struck me is [something specific — a technical challenge they've written about, a scale achievement, a product direction]. I read your engineering blog post about [specific topic] — that's exactly the kind of problem I find interesting because [genuine reason related to your background].

I also noticed you're using [tech stack element from job description] — I've been working with that extensively, so I'd expect to be productive quickly. And [something you genuinely want to learn that they offer].

Is there anything about the team structure or roadmap that you'd add that I might not have picked up from public information?"

The final question is key: Asking a follow-up signals genuine curiosity, not just recitation of facts you Googled.
followUpAnswers.What makes you a better fit than other c:
This is uncomfortable because it asks you to compare yourself to people you haven't met. The right approach is not to put others down, but to confidently articulate your specific value proposition — what you bring that is distinctive and relevant to this role.

Principles:

  • Don't speculate about other candidates — you don't know them
  • Focus entirely on your own specific strengths and how they map to this role's needs
  • Be concrete — "I have strong communication skills" is weak; "I've built X and can show you the result" is strong
  • Reference the job description — align your pitch to what they actually said they need

Sample answer:

"I can't speak to other candidates, but I can tell you what I think makes me particularly well-suited for this specific role.

You're looking for someone who can work across the full .NET stack — API, background services, and some frontend work. That's exactly what I've done for the past few years. In FolioTrack, I built the entire system: the ASP.NET Core 8 API, the EF Core data layer with SQLite, and the Angular 19 frontend. I understand how the pieces connect at a systems level, not just how to work within one layer.

I also notice the role mentions working in a small team where engineers need to be self-directed. I've worked in that environment — I'm used to owning features from design to deployment without a lot of scaffolding, and I know how to communicate progress and blockers clearly without hand-holding.

And concretely — I'm not starting from zero on your stack. I can read your codebase, contribute a PR in the first week, and ask useful questions rather than basic ones from week one."

Key: End with something concrete and actionable — a specific contribution you can make, a skill you can demonstrate, or a question that shows you're already thinking about the role practically.

questions.tip: Spend 30 minutes researching before every interview. LinkedIn, company engineering blog, Glassdoor, recent news. Mention something specific you found. It takes candidates from forgettable to memorable.

questions.question: How do you keep yourself updated with new .NET technologies?
questions.answer:
This tests whether you're passionate about the craft and proactive about growth.

Genuine sources I follow:

  • .NET Blog (dotnet.microsoft.com/blog) — official announcements and deep dives
  • Scott Hanselman's blog — practical .NET tips from inside Microsoft
  • YouTube: Nick Chapsas (clean architecture, new features), Tim Corey, Raw Coding
  • Microsoft Learn / docs.microsoft.com — official documentation for new APIs
  • GitHub: Follow .NET runtime repo, ASP.NET Core repo — see what's coming in preview
  • Dev.to, Medium — community articles about real-world usage

Active learning methods:

  • Build small side projects with new features (that's how I tried out Minimal APIs and .NET 8's primary constructors)
  • Contribute to personal projects to experiment with new patterns
  • Read source code of popular libraries (I've learned a lot reading Polly's and EF Core's internals)

"The .NET release cycle (one major version per year) gives a natural rhythm — I always spend time with the What's New page for each release and try the highlighted features in a sandbox project."
followUps: What's a recent .NET feature you're excited about?
What would you build if you had unlimited time?

followUpAnswers.What's a recent .NET feature you're exci:
This question rewards people who actually follow the ecosystem — not just those who claim to. Name something specific, explain what it does, and say why it matters to you practically. Don't pick something too obscure (shows off but doesn't connect) or too generic ("I like the performance improvements").

Strong options to mention (pick whichever you genuinely find interesting):

.NET Aspire (GA in .NET 8)
"I'm genuinely excited about .NET Aspire. It provides an opinionated stack for building cloud-native, observable .NET applications — it handles service discovery, health checks, distributed tracing, and the local development orchestration of multiple services with a dashboard. For someone who's been setting up all of that manually with Docker Compose and custom middleware, Aspire represents a huge reduction in boilerplate for microservices projects. I've been using it in a sandbox project to orchestrate an API, a worker service, and a Redis cache — the experience is significantly cleaner than what I was doing before."

Primary constructors in C# 12
"C# 12's primary constructors let you declare constructor parameters directly on the class declaration — you get field-like syntax without the ceremony of explicit constructor bodies and backing field assignments. For services with many injected dependencies, this is a real ergonomic improvement. It's a small feature but it removes noise from the most common pattern in ASP.NET Core code."

Interceptors in C# 12 (preview)
"Interceptors are a low-level but fascinating C# 12 feature — they let you replace a specific method call site at compile time without changing the original method. EF Core uses them internally for query compilation. I find it interesting as a foundation for source-generation-driven frameworks."

How to structure your answer: What the feature is → What problem it solves → How you've used it or plan to → Why it matters for the kinds of applications you build.
followUpAnswers.What would you build if you had unlimite:
This question is genuinely open-ended — it reveals your curiosity, the kinds of problems you find interesting, and your technical ambitions beyond immediate job requirements. There's no wrong answer as long as it's authentic and you can speak to it with genuine enthusiasm.

Good answer qualities:

  • Specific enough to be real (not just "a social network")
  • Technically interesting — shows you think beyond basic CRUD
  • Reflects genuine curiosity, not what you think will impress
  • Connects to something you care about (a domain problem, a tool gap, a learning goal)

Sample answer options (choose what fits you):

Technical tool: "I'd build a developer-focused personal finance system that integrates directly with brokerage APIs to pull real transaction data, runs custom analysis (like sector exposure, dividend projections, rebalancing suggestions), and serves everything through a clean API I could query however I wanted. The product equivalents (Mint, Personal Capital) are good, but they're black boxes — I want to own the data model and build the analytics I actually care about. It's also a great excuse to properly implement event sourcing, which I want to go deep on."

Domain problem: "I'd build an offline-capable tool for field technicians — the kind of people who need to capture inspection data in warehouses or buildings where there's no reliable internet. The offline-first sync problem is genuinely hard: conflict resolution, local storage, sync strategies. I've thought about this a lot and never had the time to implement it properly. I'd use .NET MAUI for the client and a custom sync API — it's the kind of distributed systems problem that's technically meaty but also practically useful."

Learning-driven: "Honestly, I'd build a distributed key-value store — something like a simplified Redis. Not because I think the world needs another one, but because implementing it would force me to truly understand distributed consensus (Raft), WAL-based persistence, and network protocol design at a level that reading about them never achieves. The best way I've found to understand a system is to build a broken version of it and fix it."

questions.tip: Name a specific recent .NET feature — .NET 9 AOT, C# 12 primary constructors, interceptors, .NET Aspire, Blazor SSR, etc. Having a concrete example shows you actually follow the ecosystem, not just claim to.
